skill.name: skywork-document
skill.description: Create structured documents in DOCX, PDF, HTML, or Markdown by sending prompts and optional reference files to the Skywork document API.
skill.summary: Generate formatted business documents from prompts and reference files using Skywork cloud services.
skill.author: SkyworkAI

security_audit.summary: Static network, credential, filesystem, and external command findings are mostly true positives in context. No confirmed malicious intent or prompt-injection attempt was found, but the skill uploads user files and prompts to Skywork APIs, stores an authentication token in a hidden home file, and launches browser commands, so it should not be published without additional human review and user-facing consent controls.

high_findings.title: Reference Files and Prompts Sent to External Cloud APIs
high_findings.description: The skill uploads user-provided files and sends document prompts to Skywork API endpoints. This is core functionality, but it can expose sensitive documents and generated content to a third-party service without an explicit in-skill consent gate.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code builds multipart uploads from local files and posts prompt payloads to remote API endpoints. The behavior is documented as intended, so confidence is high that the data-transfer risk is real even though it is not covert.

high_findings.title: Persistent Authentication Token Stored in Hidden Home File
high_findings.description: The authentication helper reads and writes Skywork tokens in ~/.skywork_token as JSON. The code does not set restrictive file permissions or use an operating system credential store, increasing exposure if the host account or backups are compromised.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token path, load path, and save path are explicit in the authentication module. The absence of permission hardening or secure storage is directly visible in the cited lines.

medium_findings.title: Environment Variables Can Redirect API Traffic
medium_findings.description: The skill allows API base URLs to be overridden through environment variables. This can be useful for testing, but a compromised or misconfigured environment could redirect authentication, file uploads, and document prompts to an unintended endpoint.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The override behavior is clearly implemented with environment reads and default production URLs. Exploitation depends on environmental control, so severity is moderate rather than critical.

medium_findings.title: Browser Launch Uses Subprocess for Login Flow
medium_findings.description: The login helper automatically launches platform browser commands. The commands are not arbitrary user commands, but the Windows branch uses shell=True with a URL, which increases command execution risk if the login base URL is influenced.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Subprocess calls are present and tied to a URL derived from configurable login settings. The normal path is legitimate browser opening, but shell invocation makes the pattern security-sensitive.

medium_findings.title: Generated and Parsed Content Written to Local Files
medium_findings.description: The scripts write parsed content to a user-selected output path and download generated documents into the current working directory. This is expected for a document tool, but it can overwrite or create local files in sensitive locations if used carelessly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write and download paths are explicit. The risk is bounded because paths come from user arguments or remote filenames, not hidden background writes.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Alerts Appear to Be False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak-cryptography locations correspond to UUID request identifiers, file download logic, argument parsing, or documentation text. No evidence found that MD5, SHA1, or another weak cryptographic algorithm is used for security decisions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review did not find weak cryptographic primitives at the flagged locations. The static pattern likely matched unrelated tokens such as UUID usage, markdown format text, or argument names.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Mostly Platform Detection
low_findings.description: The system-reconnaissance findings are primarily imports, platform detection, file existence checks, and current-directory use. No evidence found of broad host enumeration or collection of system inventory.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited code checks the operating system to open a browser and validates expected files. These are legitimate support behaviors, with no semantic evidence of reconnaissance beyond what the workflow needs.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Triggered Many External Command Alerts
low_findings.description: Most SKILL.md external-command findings are fenced usage examples that tell the agent how to invoke the included Python scripts. They are not executable code inside the skill file, but they do instruct execution of networked scripts and should remain visible in the risk profile.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The repeated alerts come from documented shell snippets rather than hidden shell execution. The commands are still operational instructions for running scripts, so the pattern is not purely benign.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Access Combined With Network Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts retrieve tokens from environment variables or a hidden file and attach them to Skywork API requests. This combination is necessary for the service but creates meaningful credential exposure risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Token acquisition and authenticated network requests are directly connected in the code paths. The pattern is confirmed, but it supports declared functionality rather than proving malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Upload to Remote Parsing Endpoint
dangerous_patterns.description: The parse script reads a local file into a multipart body and posts it to a remote parsing endpoint. This can expose confidential source documents to the external service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The upload implementation is explicit and complete. It reads the full file and sends it over the network with an authentication token.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Process Launch During Authentication
dangerous_patterns.description: The authentication script launches operating-system browser commands during login. This increases operational risk compared with printing a URL only.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess launches are clearly present and part of login automation. They are constrained to browser-opening commands, so confidence in command-injection impact is lower than confidence in command execution.

content.value_statement: Long document creation often requires structure, formatting, and source material handling. This skill sends prompts and optional reference files to Skywork to generate DOCX, PDF, HTML, or Markdown outputs.
actual_capabilities: Authenticates with Skywork using an environment token, cached token, or browser login flow.
Uploads one reference file at a time for server-side parsing and file ID creation.
Creates documents from a title, detailed content prompt, language, format, and optional file IDs.
Streams progress events from the Skywork API during parsing and document generation.
Downloads generated documents locally when a file URL is returned.
Supports DOCX, PDF, HTML, and Markdown output formats.
limitations: Requires network access and a valid Skywork account or token.
Uploads reference files and document prompts to Skywork cloud services.
Generation can take several minutes for complex documents.
Does not provide local-only document generation or offline file parsing.

use_cases.title: Prepare Client Reports
use_cases.description: Turn source PDFs, spreadsheets, and research notes into a structured report with a requested format and language.

use_cases.title: Create Internal Briefings
use_cases.description: Generate concise office documents from planning notes, uploaded references, and current context for stakeholder review.

use_cases.title: Draft Research Summaries
use_cases.description: Use uploaded materials and a detailed prompt to produce formatted summaries, outlines, or review documents.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Document
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a document from a prompt only.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a DOCX document titled Project_Update. Use English. Summarize the current project status, milestones, risks, and next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Document From File
prompt_templates.prompt: Parse the attached report, then create a PDF titled Market_Summary. Include an executive summary, key findings, risks, and recommendations.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Source Brief
prompt_templates.scenario: Combine several reference files into one document.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use these reference files to create an HTML briefing titled Vendor_Comparison. Compare vendors by pricing, capabilities, risks, and implementation effort.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Structured Report
prompt_templates.scenario: Specify format, language, structure, and audience.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Markdown report titled Expansion_Plan in Spanish for senior leadership. Include market context, assumptions, financial considerations, operational risks, and a decision matrix.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a DOCX project plan from a short set of requirements.
output_examples.output: The skill returns a Skywork download link, the local file path, and a generated project plan with sections for goals, timeline, owners, and risks.

output_examples.input: A user provides a PDF and asks for a client-ready summary report.
output_examples.output: The skill parses the file, creates a report from the returned file ID, and provides the remote link plus the saved local document path.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a Markdown market brief in another language.
output_examples.output: The skill sends the selected language and format to Skywork, streams progress, and returns the completed Markdown document location.

best_practices: Use clear titles without spaces and specify the desired output format before generation.
Warn users before uploading confidential files or prompts to Skywork cloud services.
Collect parsed file IDs carefully when combining multiple reference files.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for short answers that can be answered directly in chat.
Do not upload sensitive files unless the user has approved third-party processing.
Do not run authentication or generation in environments where token caching is not acceptable.

faq.question: Does this skill work offline?
faq.answer: No. It requires network access to Skywork APIs for authentication, parsing, and document generation.

faq.question: Which output formats are supported?
faq.answer: The skill supports DOCX, PDF, HTML, and Markdown outputs through the Skywork document API.

faq.question: Can it use uploaded files?
faq.answer: Yes. It can parse reference files one at a time and pass the returned file IDs into document generation.

faq.question: Where is the login token stored?
faq.answer: The authentication script stores token data in a hidden home file named ~/.skywork_token unless an environment token is used.

faq.question: Can users choose the document language?
faq.answer: Yes. The create script accepts a language option and sends it to the Skywork API.

faq.question: Is this safe for confidential documents?
faq.answer: Use caution. The skill uploads source files and prompts to Skywork, so users should approve external processing first.
